title: Border Patrol Fatally Shoots U.S. Citizen Alex Pretti in Minneapolis
excerpt: A Border Patrol agent shot and killed Alex Jeffrey Pretti, 37, an ICU nurse, in Minneapolis — the second fatal shooting of a U.S. citizen by federal immigration agents in the city in 17 days. Multiple eyewitness videos contradicted the DHS account that agents acted in self-defense.
content:
On January 24, 2026, Border Patrol agents fatally shot Alex Jeffrey Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive care nurse, in Minneapolis. The killing was the second fatal shooting of a U.S. citizen by federal immigration officers in the city in under three weeks, following the January 7 death of Renee Good.

  • DHS Secretary Noem and Border Patrol Commander Gregory Bovino initially claimed Pretti had approached agents with a firearm and 'violently resisted' disarmament, calling the shooting an act of self-defense.
  • At least four eyewitness videos verified by NBC News contradicted the administration's account; video showed Pretti holding a phone, not a gun, and checking on a woman who had been pushed by agents.
  • ProPublica identified the two agents who fired as Border Patrol agent Jesus Ochoa and CBP officer Raymundo Gutierrez, both assigned to Operation Metro Surge.
  • Minnesota state investigators accused federal agents of physically blocking access to the crime scene; a state court granted a temporary restraining order barring destruction of evidence.
  • Senior Trump aide Stephen Miller initially called Pretti a 'would-be assassin' but later acknowledged agents 'may not have been following' protocol for confronting bystanders.
  • Governor Tim Walz publicly blamed 'untrained' federal agents; Minneapolis police chief Brian O'Hara noted two of the city's three 2026 homicides had been carried out by federal immigration officers.

The DOJ Civil Rights Division opened an investigation after a week of national protests, but as of April 2026 NPR reported that Minnesota authorities said the federal probe had shown little sign of progress and that key evidence — including Pretti's phone and Good's car — remained inaccessible to state investigators.
mediaPlaceholder: Eyewitness video of the moments before and during the Pretti shooting, showing agents and Pretti on the street
imageAlt: Border Patrol Fatally Shoots U.S. Citizen Alex Pretti in Minneapolis
